title: Fesenjan
subtitle: Persian walnut and pomegranate chicken stew
excerpt: A rich, dark Persian stew made from ground walnuts and pomegranate molasses, simmered with chicken until the sauce is thick, deep brown, and intensely savoury-sweet. The stew has no parallel in any other cuisine — the combination of ground walnuts, tart pomegranate molasses, and poultry creates something unique: oily, complex, and unlike anything else.
sourceNotes: Fesenjan is one of the most celebrated dishes of Persian cuisine, documented in Margaret Shaida's 'The Legendary Cuisine of Persia' (1992) and Najmieh Batmanglij's 'Food of Life' (1986). It is traditionally made with duck in Gilan province in northern Iran, though chicken is now the most common version. Pomegranate molasses and walnuts are the defining ingredients — both are essential and neither can be substituted.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the cooked stew for up to 3 months. The walnut sauce separates slightly on reheating — stir vigorously over low heat to recombine.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make a day ahead and reheat gently. The flavour improves overnight as the walnuts and pomegranate molasses deepen.

content.text: The walnuts must be finely ground — not to a paste but to a very coarse meal. This is done in a food processor in short pulses. The ground walnuts are toasted in a dry pan before the liquid is added, which deepens their flavour and helps the sauce develop its characteristic dark colour. The sauce then simmers slowly for at least an hour, during which time it thickens and darkens considerably. It is ready when it is almost black and coats a spoon heavily.

The balance between the tart pomegranate molasses and the richness of the walnuts needs adjusting to taste. The stew should be distinctly tart, not sweet. If the molasses is very sweet, add a squeeze of lemon to sharpen it. If it is too sour, a teaspoon of honey or sugar balances it. Serve over plain white rice, with the cooking juices spooned over.

items.prepNote: finely ground in a food processor

content.text: Brown the chicken and make the walnut base

Heat the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} in a large heavy pan. Brown the {{chicken-thigh}} pieces for 3 to 4 minutes per side. Remove. In the same pan, cook the {{onion}} for 10 minutes until soft and golden. Add the {{turmeric}} and {{cinnamon-ground}} and cook for 1 minute. Add the ground {{walnuts}} and toast, stirring, for 3 to 4 minutes until fragrant and slightly darker.

Pour 500 ml water into the walnut mixture and bring to a simmer. Add the {{pomegranate-molasses}} and season with salt. Return the chicken to the pan. Simmer, partially covered, for 55 to 60 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the sauce is thick, dark, and glossy. If it thickens too quickly, add a little more water. Taste and adjust: add more pomegranate molasses for tartness, or a little {{caster-sugar}} if too sharp. Scatter {{pomegranate-seeds}} over to serve.

items.symptom: Sauce is oily and the oil is separating
items.cause: Walnuts are very oily, especially fresh ones
items.fix: This is normal — the oil from the walnuts separates as the sauce reduces. Stir vigorously to recombine. Using walnuts that have been toasted first helps emulsify the sauce

items.symptom: Sauce is pale brown after an hour
items.cause: Not simmered long enough or heat too low
items.fix: Fesenjan needs a genuine slow simmer — the Maillard reactions in the walnut mixture take time to develop. Keep the simmer active and cover only partially so the liquid reduces
Brands vary significantly in sweetness and acidity. Add a teaspoon of sugar or honey and taste again. A little cinnamon can also soften the perception of acidity
Ground walnuts should be a fine meal, similar to ground almonds. Pulse until no large pieces remain — about 20 to 25 short pulses in a food processor

content.text: Fesenjan originates in the Gilan and Mazandoran provinces on the Caspian coast of northern Iran, where pomegranates and walnuts grow in abundance. The original version used wild duck. The sauce is sometimes described as tasting like the distilled essence of autumn: dark, rich, bittersweet. It is one of the khoresh (stews) served at Persian New Year and at celebratory occasions. Outside Iran, fesenjan has become the best-known example of Persian cuisine, partly because its flavour profile (walnut and pomegranate) is distinctive enough to be immediately memorable. There is no analogous sauce in any other major food tradition.
